summary: Reis argues that as classical data problems become easier to solve, data professionals can now focus on higher-value 'boring' business problems and the next frontier of integrating ML/AI into business workflows. The post is a casual weekend roundup mixing this thesis with industry commentary and personal updates.
key_points: Classical data infrastructure problems are becoming commoditized
This commoditization enables focus on business value and AI/ML integration
The 'Transformer squeeze' suggests we're near the end of current ML paradigm gains
Many overvalued data companies face a coming reckoning
money_quotes: Boring is Back! Solving the classical data problems is getting simpler, and this allows us to move up the value chain to solve 'boring' problems.
I sometimes joke we're in a 'classical ML/deep learning winter.'
Lots of dog shit companies out there about to face a day of reckoning.
Tim wasn't delivering software; Tim was delivering a team that was delivering software.
key_insight: The maturation of data infrastructure means the real work now shifts from technical plumbing to solving actual business problems and integrating AI into workflows.
